title: St. Louis City Region Overview
content:
St. Louis City occupies ~62 square miles at the geographic center of the metro area. Independent municipality separate from St. Louis County. Population ~278,000. Cultural and economic hub of eastern Missouri. Most destinations reachable in 15-20 minutes.

79 Official Neighborhoods including:
- Benton Park: Updated historic red brick, bars and restaurants
- Central West End: Urban streets, historic mansions, Forest Park proximity
- Downtown: Industrial lofts and urban living
- Lafayette Square: Historic painted brick homes
- Soulard: Brick homes, bars, restaurants, coffee shops
- The Grove: Historic charm with new development and nightlife

History: Founded 1764 by Pierre Laclede and Auguste Chouteau. Named after King Louis IX. Gateway to the West during 19th century industrial boom. Fourth-largest U.S. city in early 1900s. Hosted 1904 World's Fair and Olympics.

Landmarks: Gateway Arch (1965), Old Courthouse, Missouri History Museum, Union Station, Busch Stadium, Missouri Botanical Garden.

Housing: Lower cost of living than most Midwest major cities. Starters under K to historic mansions over M. Classic red brick, Victorian, townhomes, modern condos.

Economy: Fortune 500 companies (Edward Jones, Reinsurance Group of America). World-class universities. Metro region ranks 23rd nationally.

Dining: Soulard, Benton Park, Central West End, The Hill among top food neighborhoods. Thriving restaurant scene comparable to much larger metros.
